name: Flirty Fishing
description: Flirty Fishing, also known as 'loving lying,' was a controversial practice employed by some members of the Children of God (later renamed The Family International), a new religious movement founded by David Berg. The practice involved female members engaging in sexual activity with non-members, often strangers, with the stated aim of converting them to the group's beliefs. Proponents viewed it as a form of evangelism, arguing that it was a way to spread God's love and recruit new members in a permissive and sexually liberated era. They often cited biblical passages interpreted to justify unconventional means of spreading the gospel. Critics, including former members and religious scholars, condemned Flirty Fishing as a form of sexual exploitation, manipulation, and prostitution. They argued that it was psychologically damaging to the women involved and morally reprehensible, exploiting vulnerable individuals for the benefit of the organization. Mainstream religious perspectives universally reject Flirty Fishing as a perversion of Christian teachings and a violation of ethical principles. Law enforcement has investigated the practice in some jurisdictions, and it contributed to the group's negative reputation and legal challenges. The practice has been widely discontinued by the group, although its legacy continues to be a point of contention and controversy.
subtitle: Controversial recruitment method of The Family.

claims.claim: Flirty Fishing was a divinely ordained method of evangelism.
claims.evidence: Interpretations of biblical passages promoting unconventional approaches to spreading the gospel and statements by David Berg.
claims.criticism: Mainstream religious interpretations condemn sexual activity outside of marriage and reject the notion that God would endorse such practices. It is seen as a gross misinterpretation of scripture.

claims.claim: It was a demonstration of God's love and acceptance.
claims.evidence: The belief that sex was a powerful and positive force that could be used to connect with others and bring them closer to God.
claims.criticism: Critics argue that it was a manipulative tactic that exploited vulnerable individuals and objectified women, contradicting the concept of genuine love and respect.

claims.claim: It was effective in recruiting new members.
claims.evidence: Testimonies from former members who claim to have been recruited through Flirty Fishing.
claims.criticism: The long-term effectiveness is debated, as many recruits left the group after becoming disillusioned with its practices. The ethical implications overshadow any potential recruitment success.

keyFigures.name: David Berg
keyFigures.role: Founder of the Children of God and proponent of Flirty Fishing

keyFigures.name: Deborah Davis
keyFigures.role: Former member and outspoken critic of the practice

mythologyConnections: Use of sexuality in religious rituals (though often distinct in intent and context)

timeline.event: Flirty Fishing becomes a prominent practice within the Children of God.
Increased scrutiny and criticism lead to a decline in the practice.
The legacy of Flirty Fishing continues to be debated and discussed in the context of cults and religious abuse.

sources.author: Davis, Deborah
sources.work: The Children of God: The Inside Story
sources.title: Davis, Deborah

sources.author: Bainbridge, William Sims
sources.work: Satan's Power: A Deviant Psychotherapy Cult
sources.title: Bainbridge, William Sims
